title: Pulse Shaping to Mitigate the Impact of Device Imperfections in Field-Free Switching Using Combined Spin-Orbit and Spin-Transfer Torques
authors: Kuldeep Ray, Jérémie Vigier, Sylvain Martin, Chloé Bouard, Nicolas Lefoulon, Marc Drouard, Gilles Gaudin
abstract: Combining spin-orbit (SOT) and spin-transfer torques (STT) provides a practical approach for field-free switching in spin-orbit torque magnetic random-access memory (SOT-MRAM), a prerequisite for industrial deployment, but can compromise reliability through phenomena such as backhopping, especially in top-pinned stacks commonly used for SOT-MRAM. We investigate the write error rate (WER) of combined SOT + STT switching in top-pinned devices that are not optimized for STT switching. Experiments reveal clear indications of STT-induced backhopping and a pronounced field-free SOT switching asymmetry between AP-to-P and P-to-AP transitions. Our macrospin model, using two coupled Landau Lifshitz Gilbert equations for the free and the reference layers, qualitatively reproduces this asymmetry and reveals an intermediate loss-of-determinism regime in addition to the well-known backhopping region. Based on these simulations, we propose mitigation strategies and experimentally demonstrate that STT pulse shaping reduces WER and improves switching robustness in the presence of device imperfections.
summary: This paper addresses the reliability issues in SOT-MRAM, specifically the problem of backhopping and switching asymmetry in field-free operation. By using both experiments and macrospin simulations, the authors identify that device imperfections like interlayer coupling and reference layer instability are the primary culprits. They propose and experimentally demonstrate that engineering the shape of the STT and SOT voltage pulses can suppress these errors. This work provides a practical pathway toward more robust and deterministic magnetic memory devices.
main_problem: Achieving reliable, field-free switching in SOT-MRAM is hindered by device imperfections like interlayer coupling and reference layer instability, which cause backhopping and switching asymmetry.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that STT pulse shaping (using a decaying pulse) and two-step SOT pulses can significantly reduce the Write Error Rate (WER) and mitigate backhopping.
method: The study combines experimental measurements of Write Error Rate (WER) in top-pinned MTJ devices with macrospin simulations using coupled Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equations.
model_or_system: A top-pinned Magnetic Tunnel Junction (MTJ) pillar consisting of a CoFeB free layer and a synthetic antiferromagnetic (SAF) reference layer, utilizing combined Spin-Orbit Torque (SOT) and Spin-Transfer Torque (STT) mechanisms.
key_observables: Write Error Rate (WER), switching asymmetry (AP-to-P vs. P-to-AP), and voltage thresholds.
important_parameters: SOT and STT pulse amplitudes, pulse duration, interlayer exchange coupling, reference layer magnetization tilt, and the 'loss-of-determinism' regime.
main_assumptions: The simulations use a macrospin approximation rather than full micromagnetics to achieve statistical significance.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the MTJ stack and hysteresis loop; Fig 2 details the experimental setup and WER procedure; Fig 3 displays WER curves showing threshold asymmetry; Fig 4 shows device instability; Fig 5 illustrates the loss-of-determinism regime; Fig 6 and 7 demonstrate the effectiveness of modified pulse shapes in reducing WER.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of field-free switching reliability, presents experimental evidence of asymmetry and backhopping, uses macrospin simulations to identify the underlying physical causes (RL tilt and coupling), proposes pulse-shaping mitigation strategies, and validates these strategies experimentally.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) Schematic of the material stack of SOT-MRAM devices measured in this study. (b) Representative TMR hysteresis loop obtained by sweeping the out-of-plane field (HZ), showing a TMR = 63% at HZ=0 and an offset field Hoff = –11.94 kA/m.
Fig. 2. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup. (b) WER definition and measurement scheme.

Fig. 3. WER as a function of SOT voltage (VSOT): (a) in the presence of an external in-plane magnetic field, HX = 63.66 kA/m, and (b) without any external magnetic field. (c) WER as a function of STT voltage (VSTT) for pulse widths of 5 ns and 10 ns.
Fig. 4. (a) Field-free WER as a function of SOT voltage (VSOT). (b) Repeat measurement on the same device under identical conditions.

Fig. 5. Simulated WER versus SOT current density using different coupling parameters for (a) AP to P and (b) P to AP transitions. Simulated WER colormaps as a function of SOT and STT current densities for AP to P and P to AP transitions under different conditions: (c-d) perpendicular RL with A0>0, (e-f) tilted RL with A0>0, and (g-h) tilted RL with A0>0 using a modified pulse (1 ns plateau and 9 ns decay).
Fig. 6. (a) WER versus VSTT for standard rectangular and modified pulse shapes. (b) WER versus VSOT for a modified two-step SOT pulse.

Fig. 7. WER versus VSOT using modified SOT and STT pulses (shown in inset) (a) for different VSTT, and (b) for different overlaps between SOT and STT pulses with VSTT = 0.77 V. A negative overlap corresponds to the separation between the end of the SOT and the beginning of the STT pulses.
